_meta.title: Sorting I — Insertion, Selection, Bubble, and Why They Lose

questions.question: What is the best-case time complexity of insertion sort on an already-sorted array?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n²)
questions.explanation: When the input is already sorted, the inner while-loop breaks immediately on every iteration, so insertion sort does exactly n−1 comparisons and zero shifts: O(n).

questions.question: A sort is 'stable' if:
options: It always runs in O(n log n) time
Equal elements keep their original relative order
It uses no extra memory
It performs the same number of comparisons on every input
questions.explanation: Stability means that if two elements compare as equal, their order in the output matches their order in the input. This matters when sorting by one key while preserving another.

questions.question: Selection sort always performs Θ(n²) comparisons, even on sorted input. Why?
options: It cannot detect that the input is sorted
The inner loop must scan the entire unsorted suffix to find the minimum
It swaps elements on every iteration
It uses recursion that cannot terminate early
questions.explanation: Selection sort has no early exit mechanism. To find the minimum in the unsorted portion, it must compare every element in that portion on every pass, yielding n(n−1)/2 comparisons regardless of input order.

questions.question: Python's sorted() uses Tim sort, which calls insertion sort internally. Why does Tim sort use insertion sort rather than merge sort for small subarrays?
options: Insertion sort has lower constant factors and no recursion overhead for small n
Insertion sort is O(n log n) for all inputs
Merge sort cannot sort small arrays
Insertion sort uses less code, reducing binary size
questions.explanation: For small n (typically n < 32), the O(n²) cost of insertion sort is dominated by low constant factors: no recursion, no auxiliary array, and excellent cache locality. The overhead of divide-and-conquer makes merge sort slower at small scales.

questions.question: Which of these O(n^2) sorting algorithms has the best performance on nearly-sorted input?
options: Selection sort
Bubble sort
Insertion sort
All are equal
questions.explanation: Insertion sort runs in O(n) on nearly-sorted data because each element only needs to shift past a few neighbors. Selection sort always scans the entire unsorted portion regardless of order, and bubble sort still needs multiple passes.
